title: Miss Edith Makes Another Friend
content:
آ آ آ آ Oh, youï؟½re the girl lives on the corner? Come in if you want to come quick!
آ آ آ آ Thereï؟½s no one but me in the house, and the cook but sheï؟½s only a stick.
آ آ آ آ Donï؟½t try the front way, but come over the fence through the window thatï؟½s how.
آ آ آ آ Donï؟½t mind the big dog he wonï؟½t bite you just see him obey me! there, now!

آ آ آ آ Whatï؟½s your name? Mary Ellen? How funny! Mineï؟½s Edith itï؟½s nicer, you see;
آ آ آ آ But yours does for you, for youï؟½re plainer, though maybe youï؟½re gooder than me;
آ آ آ آ For Jack says Iï؟½m sometimes a devil, but Jack, of all folks, neednï؟½t talk,
آ آ آ آ For I donï؟½t call the seamstress an angel till Ma says the poor thing must ï؟½walk.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ Come in! Itï؟½s quite dark in the parlor, for sister will keep the blinds down,
آ آ آ آ For you know her complexion is sallow like yours, but she isnï؟½t as brown;
آ آ آ آ Though Jack says that isnï؟½t the reason she likes to sit here with Jim Moore.
آ آ آ آ Do you think that he meant that she kissed him? Would you if your lips wasnï؟½t sore?

آ آ آ آ If you like, you can try our piano. ï؟½Tainï؟½t ours. A man left it here
آ آ آ آ To rent by the month, although Ma says he hasnï؟½t been paid for a year.
آ آ آ آ Sister plays oh, such fine variations! why, I once heard a gentleman say
آ آ آ آ That she didnï؟½t mind that for the music in fact, it was just in her way!

آ آ آ آ Ainï؟½t I funny? And yet itï؟½s the queerest of all that, whatever I say,
آ آ آ آ One half of the folks die a-laughing, and the rest, they all look tï؟½other way.
آ آ آ آ And some say, ï؟½That child!ï؟½ Do they ever say that to such people as you?
آ آ آ آ Though maybe youï؟½re naturally silly, and that makes your eyes so askew.

آ آ آ آ Now stop donï؟½t you dare to be crying! Just as sure as you live, if you do,
آ آ آ آ Iï؟½ll call in my big dog to bite you, and Iï؟½ll make my Papa kill you, too!
آ آ آ آ And then whereï؟½ll you be? So play pretty. Thereï؟½s my doll, and a nice piece of cake.
آ آ آ آ You donï؟½t want it you think it is poison! Then Iï؟½ll eat it, dear, just for your sake!
meaning:
This evocative piece by Bret Harte (Francis) represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Bret Harte (Francis)'s bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
